blocks.body: Say / Tell / Ask shows how to choose the right reporting verb for a statement, a question, or a request.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"In reported speech, the right choice between say, tell, and ask makes the sentence natural and grammatically correct.","rules":[{"label":"say","color":"emerald","text":"say usually does not need a direct object before the reported clause.","example":"She said that she was tired."},{"label":"tell","color":"blue","text":"tell usually needs an object because you say who received the information.","example":"She told me that she was tired."},{"label":"ask","color":"amber","text":"ask is used for questions, requests, and polite appeals.","example":"He asked if I was ready. / She asked me to wait."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. The main difference
blocks.body:
• say + (that)-clause: the focus is on the content.
  • tell + object + (that)-clause: it matters who heard the message.
  • ask + question clause: for reported questions.
  • ask + object + to-infinitive: for reported requests.

Because of this, She said me is wrong, while She told me is correct.

blocks.heading: 2. Usage examples
blocks.body:
FunctionNatural choiceExample
Statementsay / tellHe said that he was busy. / He told me that he was busy.
QuestionaskShe asked where I lived.
RequestaskThey asked us to come early.
Instructiontell / orderThe coach told us to keep moving.

blocks.heading: 3. Common mistakes
blocks.body:
• Mistake: She said me that...
    Better: She told me that... or She said that...
  • Mistake: using tell for a reported question.
    Better: He asked if I was ready.
  • Mistake: forgetting the object after tell.
    Better: The teacher told us to listen.

blocks.heading: 4. Quick rule
blocks.body:
• say = report the content.
  • tell = say something to someone, so an object is usually needed.
  • ask = ask a question or make a request.
